Nitrogen, sulphur, halogens, and phosphorus are detected using Lassaigne’s test. The organic compound is fused with sodium metal to convert covalent elements into water-soluble ionic forms: sodium cyanide ($NaCN$), sodium sulphide ($Na_2S$), and sodium halides ($NaX$).
$$Na + C + N \xrightarrow{\Delta} NaCN$$
$$2Na + S \xrightarrow{\Delta} Na_2S$$
$$Na + X \xrightarrow{\Delta} NaX \text{ (X = Cl, Br, or I)}$$
The fused mass is extracted with distilled water to prepare the 'sodium fusion extract'.

subsections.title: Test for Nitrogen
subsections.content_text:
The sodium fusion extract is boiled with iron(II) sulphate ($FeSO_4$) and acidified with concentrated sulphuric acid. $NaCN$ reacts with $Fe^{2+}$ to form sodium hexacyanidoferrate(II). Some $Fe^{2+}$ is oxidized to $Fe^{3+}$, which then reacts to form Prussian blue colored iron(III) hexacyanidoferrate(II).
$$6CN^- + Fe^{2+} \rightarrow [Fe(CN)_6]^{4-}$$
$$3[Fe(CN)_6]^{4-} + 4Fe^{3+} \xrightarrow{xH_2O} Fe_4[Fe(CN)_6]_3 \cdot xH_2O$$

subsections.title: Test for Sulphur
subsections.content_text:
Sulphur can be detected by two methods:
(a) Acidifying the extract with acetic acid and adding lead acetate, which forms a black precipitate of lead sulphide ($PbS$):
$$S^{2-} + Pb^{2+} \rightarrow PbS \text{ (Black)}$$
(b) Adding sodium nitroprusside to the extract, which produces a violet colour:
$$S^{2-} + [Fe(CN)_5NO]^{2-} \rightarrow [Fe(CN)_5NOS]^{4-} \text{ (Violet)}$$
If both N and S are present, sodium thiocyanate ($NaSCN$) forms, giving a blood-red colour with $Fe^{3+}$. If excess sodium is used during fusion, $NaSCN$ decomposes into $NaCN$ and $Na_2S$.

subsections.title: Test for Halogens
subsections.content_text:
The sodium fusion extract is acidified with nitric acid and treated with silver nitrate ($AgNO_3$).
$$X^- + Ag^+ \rightarrow AgX$$
- White precipitate soluble in $NH_4OH$ indicates Chlorine ($Cl$).
- Yellowish precipitate sparingly soluble in $NH_4OH$ indicates Bromine ($Br$).
- Yellow precipitate insoluble in $NH_4OH$ indicates Iodine ($I$).
If N or S are present, the extract must be boiled with concentrated $HNO_3$ first to remove $CN^-$ and $S^{2-}$ ions, which would otherwise interfere by forming precipitates with $AgNO_3$.

subsections.title: Test for Phosphorus
subsections.content_text:
The compound is heated with sodium peroxide to oxidize phosphorus to phosphate. The solution is boiled with nitric acid and treated with ammonium molybdate. A yellow precipitate of ammonium phosphomolybdate confirms phosphorus.
$$Na_3PO_4 + 3HNO_3 \rightarrow H_3PO_4 + 3NaNO_3$$
$$H_3PO_4 + 12(NH_4)_2MoO_4 + 21HNO_3 \rightarrow (NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3 + 21NH_4NO_3 + 12H_2O$$

subsections.title: Introduction to Quantitative Analysis
subsections.content_text: Quantitative analysis determines the mass percentage of each element in a compound, which is essential for calculating empirical and molecular formulas.

subsections.title: Estimation of Carbon and Hydrogen
subsections.content_text:
A known mass of an organic compound is burnt in excess oxygen and copper(II) oxide. Carbon is converted to $CO_2$ and hydrogen to $H_2O$. The masses of $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ produced are used to calculate the percentages of C and H.
$$C_xH_y + (x + y/4) O_2 \rightarrow x CO_2 + (y/2) H_2O$$ The mass of water produced is determined by passing the mixture through a weighed U-tube containing anhydrous calcium chloride. Carbon dioxide is absorbed in another U-tube containing concentrated solution of potassium hydroxide. These tubes are connected in series (Fig. 8.14). The increase in masses of calcium chloride and potassium hydroxide gives the amounts of water and carbon dioxide from which the percentages of carbon and hydrogen are calculated. Let the mass of organic compound be $m$ g, mass of water and carbon dioxide produced be $m_1$ and $m_2$ g respectively:

Percentage of carbon = $\frac{12 \times m_2 \times 100}{44 \times m}$

Percentage of hydrogen = $\frac{2 \times m_1 \times 100}{18 \times m}$

diagrams.label: Fig. 8.14
diagrams.description: Apparatus for the estimation of carbon and hydrogen. It shows a combustion tube containing a sample in a platinum boat and CuO pallets heated by a burner. Pure dry oxygen is passed through. The products pass through a U-tube with anhydrous $CaCl_2$ (to absorb water) and then a U-tube with KOH solution (to absorb $CO_2$). Excess $O_2$ exits at the end.

subsections.title: Worked Example 8.20
subsections.content_text: Calculation of the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in an organic compound based on the masses of $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ produced during complete combustion.

worked_examples.label: Problem 8.20
worked_examples.problem: On complete combustion, 0.246 g of an organic compound gave 0.198g of carbon dioxide and 0.1014g of water. Determine the percentage composition of carbon and hydrogen in the compound.
worked_examples.solution:
Percentage of carbon = $\frac{12 \times 0.198 \times 100}{44 \times 0.246} = 21.95\%$
Percentage of hydrogen = $\frac{2 \times 0.1014 \times 100}{18 \times 0.246} = 4.58\%$

subsections.title: Nitrogen - Dumas Method
subsections.content_text:
There are two methods for estimation of nitrogen: (i) Dumas method and (ii) Kjeldahl’s method. In the Dumas method, the nitrogen containing organic compound, when heated with copper oxide in an atmosphere of carbon dioxide, yields free nitrogen in addition to carbon dioxide and water.
$$C_xH_yN_z + (2x + y/2) CuO \rightarrow x CO_2 + (y/2) H_2O + (z/2) N_2 + (2x + y/2) Cu$$
Traces of nitrogen oxides formed, if any, are reduced to nitrogen by passing the gaseous mixture over a heated copper gauze. The mixture of gases so produced is collected over an aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide which absorbs carbon dioxide. Nitrogen is collected in the upper part of the graduated tube (Fig. 8.15).

Let the mass of organic compound = $m$ g
Volume of nitrogen collected = $V_1$ mL
Room temperature = $T_1$ K
Volume of nitrogen at STP = $\frac{P_1 V_1 \times 273}{760 \times T_1}$ (Let it be $V$ mL)
Where $P_1$ and $V_1$ are the pressure and volume of nitrogen. $P_1$ is obtained by the relation: $P_1 = \text{Atmospheric pressure} - \text{Aqueous tension}$.
Since 22400 mL $N_2$ at STP weighs 28 g, $V$ mL $N_2$ at STP weighs = $\frac{28 \times V}{22400}$ g.
Percentage of nitrogen = $\frac{28 \times V \times 100}{22400 \times m}$

diagrams.label: Fig. 8.15
diagrams.description: Dumas method apparatus. A furnace contains a tube with $CO_2$ inlet, CuO gauze, organic compound mixed with CuO, coarse CuO, and reduced copper gauze. The gases pass through a mercury seal into a nitrometer filled with KOH solution. Nitrogen gas collects at the top of the graduated nitrometer.

subsections.title: Worked Example 8.21
subsections.content_text: Numerical application of the Dumas method to calculate the percentage of nitrogen, including corrections for aqueous tension and conversion to STP volume.

worked_examples.label: Problem 8.21
worked_examples.problem: In Dumas’ method for estimation of nitrogen, 0.3g of an organic compound gave 50mL of nitrogen collected at 300K temperature and 715mm pressure. Calculate the percentage composition of nitrogen in the compound. (Aqueous tension at 300K=15 mm)
worked_examples.solution:
Volume of nitrogen collected at 300K and 715mm pressure is 50 mL.
Actual pressure = $715 - 15 = 700$ mm.
Volume of nitrogen at STP = $\frac{273 \times 700 \times 50}{300 \times 760} = 41.9$ mL.
22,400 mL of $N_2$ at STP weighs = 28 g.
41.9 mL of nitrogen weighs = $\frac{28 \times 41.9}{22400}$ g.
Percentage of nitrogen = $\frac{28 \times 41.9 \times 100}{22400 \times 0.3} = 17.46\%$

subsections.title: Nitrogen - Kjeldahl's Method
subsections.content_text:
In Kjeldahl's method, the nitrogen-containing compound is heated with concentrated sulphuric acid, converting nitrogen to ammonium sulphate. The mixture is then heated with excess sodium hydroxide to liberate ammonia gas, which is absorbed in a known excess of standard sulphuric acid. The amount of ammonia is determined by back-titrating the unreacted acid with a standard alkali.

Chemical Reactions:
1. $\text{Organic compound} + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4$
2. $(NH_4)_2SO_4 + 2NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2NH_3 + 2H_2O$
3. $2NH_3 + H_2SO_4 \rightarrow (NH_4)_2SO_4$

Derivation:
Let mass of organic compound = $m$ g. Volume of $H_2SO_4$ (molarity $M$) taken = $V$ mL. Volume of $NaOH$ (molarity $M$) used for titration of excess acid = $V_1$ mL.
$V_1$ mL of $NaOH$ ($M$) = $V_1/2$ mL of $H_2SO_4$ ($M$).
Volume of $H_2SO_4$ ($M$) reacted with $NH_3 = (V - V_1/2)$ mL.
$(V - V_1/2)$ mL of $H_2SO_4$ ($M$) = $2(V - V_1/2)$ mL of $NH_3$ ($M$).
1000 mL of 1 M $NH_3$ contains 14 g of N.
Percentage of $N = \frac{1.4 \times M \times 2(V - V_1/2)}{m}$

Limitations: This method is not applicable to compounds containing nitrogen in nitro and azo groups or nitrogen in a ring (e.g., pyridine), as these do not convert to ammonium sulphate under these conditions.

diagrams.label: Fig. 8.16
diagrams.description: Kjeldahl method apparatus showing two stages: 1. Digestion: Heating the organic compound with conc. $H_2SO_4$ and $CuSO_4$ in a Kjeldahl flask. 2. Distillation: The digested contents are mixed with NaOH in a round-bottom flask with a Kjeldahl trap. Liberated ammonia is distilled through a condenser and absorbed into a flask containing a known volume of standard acid.

subsections.title: Worked Example 8.22
subsections.content_text:
Calculation of nitrogen percentage using Kjeldahl's method data.
The final calculation for the percentage of nitrogen in the organic compound is as follows: Mass of nitrogen = $\frac{14 \times 20}{1000}$ g. Percentage of nitrogen = $\frac{14 \times 20 \times 100}{1000 \times 0.5} = 56.0\%$.

worked_examples.label: Problem 8.22
worked_examples.problem: During estimation of nitrogen present in an organic compound by Kjeldahl’s method, the ammonia evolved from 0.5 g of the compound in Kjeldahl’s estimation of nitrogen, neutralized 10 mL of 1 M $H_2SO_4$. Find out the percentage of nitrogen in the compound.
worked_examples.solution:
1 M of 10 mL $H_2SO_4 = 1M$ of 20 mL $NH_3$
1000 mL of 1M ammonia contains 14 g nitrogen
20 mL of 1M ammonia contains = 0.28 g. Percentage of nitrogen = (0.28 × 100) / 0.5 = 56.0%

subsections.title: 8.10.3 Halogens - Carius Method
subsections.content_text: The Carius method is used for the estimation of halogens in an organic compound. A known mass of the organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid in the presence of silver nitrate contained in a hard glass tube known as a Carius tube (Fig. 8.17). During this process, carbon and hydrogen present in the compound are oxidised to carbon dioxide and water. The halogen present reacts with silver nitrate to form the corresponding silver halide (AgX). The silver halide is then filtered, washed, dried, and weighed.

diagrams.label: Fig. 8.17
diagrams.description: Apparatus for the Carius method. It consists of a long hard glass tube (Carius tube) approximately 50 cm in length and 2 cm in diameter. Inside the tube, a sealed capillary contains the organic compound, which is surrounded by fuming nitric acid (HNO3) and silver nitrate (AgNO3).

subsections.title: 8.10.3 Halogens - Quantitative Calculation
subsections.content_text: To calculate the percentage of halogen, let the mass of the organic compound taken be $m$ g and the mass of silver halide (AgX) formed be $m_1$ g. Since 1 mole of AgX contains 1 mole of halogen (X), the mass of halogen in $m_1$ g of AgX is given by: $\text{Mass of halogen} = \frac{\text{atomic mass of X} \times m_1}{\text{molecular mass of AgX}}$. The percentage of halogen is then: $\text{Percentage of halogen} = \frac{\text{atomic mass of X} \times m_1 \times 100}{\text{molecular mass of AgX} \times m}$.

subsections.title: Worked Example 8.23
subsections.content_text: This example illustrates the application of the Carius method for estimating the percentage of bromine in an organic compound.

worked_examples.label: Problem 8.23
worked_examples.problem: In Carius method of estimation of halogen, 0.15 g of an organic compound gave 0.12 g of AgBr. Find out the percentage of bromine in the compound.
worked_examples.solution: Molar mass of AgBr = $108 + 80 = 188$ g mol$^{-1}$. 188 g AgBr contains 80 g bromine. Therefore, 0.12 g AgBr contains $\frac{80 \times 0.12}{188}$ g bromine. Percentage of bromine = $\frac{80 \times 0.12 \times 100}{188 \times 0.15} = 34.04\%$.

subsections.title: 8.10.4 Sulphur - Estimation Method
subsections.content_text: In the estimation of sulphur, a known mass of an organic compound is heated in a Carius tube with sodium peroxide or fuming nitric acid. The sulphur present in the compound is oxidised to sulphuric acid. This is then precipitated as barium sulphate ($BaSO_4$) by adding an excess of barium chloride solution in water. The resulting precipitate is filtered, washed, dried, and weighed. The percentage of sulphur is then calculated from the mass of barium sulphate obtained.

subsections.title: 8.10.4 Sulphur - Quantitative Calculation
subsections.content_text: Let the mass of the organic compound taken be $m$ g and the mass of barium sulphate formed be $m_1$ g. Since 1 mole of $BaSO_4$ ($233$ g) contains 32 g of sulphur, $m_1$ g of $BaSO_4$ contains $\frac{32 \times m_1}{233}$ g of sulphur. The percentage of sulphur is calculated as: $\text{Percentage of sulphur} = \frac{32 \times m_1 \times 100}{233 \times m}$.

subsections.title: Worked Example 8.24
subsections.content_text: This example demonstrates the calculation of sulphur percentage based on the mass of barium sulphate precipitate.

worked_examples.label: Problem 8.24
worked_examples.problem: In sulphur estimation, 0.157 g of an organic compound gave 0.4813 g of barium sulphate. What is the percentage of sulphur in the compound?
worked_examples.solution: Molecular mass of $BaSO_4 = 137 + 32 + 64 = 233$ g. 233 g $BaSO_4$ contains 32 g sulphur. 0.4813 g $BaSO_4$ contains $\frac{32 \times 0.4813}{233}$ g sulphur. Percentage of sulphur = $\frac{32 \times 0.4813 \times 100}{233 \times 0.157} = 42.10\%$.

subsections.title: 8.10.5 Phosphorus - Estimation Method
subsections.content_text: A known mass of an organic compound is heated with fuming nitric acid, which oxidises the phosphorus present to phosphoric acid. This can be precipitated as ammonium phosphomolybdate, $(NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3$, by adding ammonia and ammonium molybdate. Alternatively, phosphoric acid may be precipitated as magnesium ammonium phosphate ($MgNH_4PO_4$) by adding magnesia mixture, which upon ignition yields magnesium pyrophosphate ($Mg_2P_2O_7$).

subsections.title: 8.10.5 Phosphorus - Quantitative Calculation
subsections.content_text: If phosphorus is estimated as ammonium phosphomolybdate, the percentage is: $\text{Percentage of phosphorus} = \frac{31 \times m_1 \times 100}{1877 \times m}$, where $m_1$ is the mass of $(NH_4)_3PO_4 \cdot 12MoO_3$ and $1877$ g is its molar mass. If estimated as $Mg_2P_2O_7$, the percentage is: $\text{Percentage of phosphorus} = \frac{62 \times m_1 \times 100}{222 \times m}$, where $m_1$ is the mass of $Mg_2P_2O_7$ formed and $222$ u is its molar mass. The factor 62 represents the mass of two phosphorus atoms present in one molecule of $Mg_2P_2O_7$.

subsections.title: 8.10.6 Oxygen - Estimation Methods
subsections.content_text: The percentage of oxygen in an organic compound is usually found by difference: subtracting the sum of the percentages of all other elements from 100. However, it can be estimated directly by decomposing a definite mass of the compound by heating in a stream of nitrogen gas. The gaseous products containing oxygen are passed over red-hot coke, converting all oxygen to carbon monoxide ($CO$). This mixture is then passed through warm iodine pentoxide ($I_2O_5$), where $CO$ is oxidised to carbon dioxide ($CO_2$), producing iodine ($I_2$). The reactions are: $2C + O_2 \xrightarrow{1373 K} 2CO$ (A) and $I_2O_5 + 5CO \rightarrow I_2 + 5CO_2$ (B).

subsections.title: 8.10.6 Oxygen - Direct Estimation Calculation
subsections.content_text: By equating the amount of $CO$ produced in reaction (A) to the amount used in reaction (B), we find that 1 mole of oxygen ($32$ g) liberated from the compound produces 2 moles of carbon dioxide ($88$ g). Let the mass of organic compound be $m$ g and the mass of $CO_2$ produced be $m_1$ g. The percentage of oxygen is: $\text{Percentage of oxygen} = \frac{32 \times m_1 \times 100}{88 \times m}$. The percentage can also be derived from the amount of iodine produced.

subsections.title: Modern Elemental Analysis
subsections.content_text: Presently, the estimation of elements (C, H, N) in an organic compound is carried out using microquantities of substances and automatic experimental techniques. An apparatus known as a CHN elemental analyser is used, which requires only 1-3 mg of the substance and displays the results on a screen within a short time.

subsections.title: Structure, Bonding and Shapes
subsections.content_text: Organic compounds are formed due to covalent bonding. Their nature is described using the orbitals hybridisation concept, where carbon can have $sp^3$, $sp^2$, and $sp$ hybridised orbitals. These lead to tetrahedral (methane), planar (ethene), and linear (ethyne) shapes. Single bonds are sigma ($\sigma$) bonds, while multiple bonds involve pi ($\pi$) bonds formed by lateral overlap of unhybridised p orbitals. 3D representations use wedge and dash formulas.

subsections.title: Classification and Nomenclature
subsections.content_text: Organic compounds are classified by structure or functional groups. A functional group is an atom or group of atoms that determines the physical and chemical properties of the compound. Systematic naming follows rules laid down by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), correlating names with structure so the reader can deduce the formula.

subsections.title: Reaction Mechanisms and Displacement Effects
subsections.content_text: Mechanisms involve the breaking and making of covalent bonds, which can occur in heterolytic (forming carbocations or carbanions) or homolytic (forming free radicals) fashion. Species are classified as nucleophiles (electron pair donors) or electrophiles (electron pair acceptors). Electron displacement effects like inductive, resonance, electromeric, and hyperconjugation effects polarise bonds. Reactions are broadly classified as substitution, addition, elimination, and rearrangement.

subsections.title: Purification and Qualitative Analysis
subsections.content_text: Purification methods include sublimation, distillation, and differential extraction based on physical property differences. Chromatography is a versatile technique for separation and identification based on differential adsorption or partitioning. Qualitative analysis involves detecting elements like nitrogen, sulphur, and halogens, often using Lassaigne's test.

subsections.title: Quantitative Analysis Summary
subsections.content_text: Quantitative analysis determines the elemental composition. Carbon and hydrogen are estimated by measuring $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ produced. Nitrogen is estimated by Dumas or Kjeldahl's method. Halogens are estimated by the Carius method, and sulphur and phosphorus are estimated by oxidising them to sulphuric and phosphoric acids, respectively. Oxygen is usually determined by difference.
